evidence_quotes.quote: the ability for video game companies to render their products unusable and revoking licenses after a purchase.
some video games can simply stop existing if their publishers decide that they don't make enough money.
There is clearly no requirement from any publisher to maintain their games in working state and keep their servers running forever.

paragraphs.text: Dear Sir or Madam, The Digital Fairness Act aims to address gaps in consumer protection online. One large such gap, which is so far missing from the proposal, is the ability for video game companies to render their products unusable and revoking licenses after a purchase. In the words of Philippe Tremblay, former director of subscriptions at Ubisoft, players should be "comfortable with not owning [their] games." As he pointed out, this is what happened with music and films, where the use of physical media like CDs and DVDs has declined in favour of subscription-based access on online platforms. Video games, like music and films, are both pieces of entertainment and works of art, and need to be both regulated as commercial products and protected as elements of culture. But unlike music or films, some video games can simply stop existing if their publishers decide that they don't make enough money. This is what famously happened with The Crew, which led to the Stop Killing Games initiative. Art and culture need to be protected from basic corporate greed. The specifics of how this should be implemented are of course very important. There is clearly no requirement from any publisher to maintain their games in working state and keep their servers running forever. However, many single-player games nowadays require users to be online for anti-consumer reasons such as making sure that they are able to access the online shop or that their user data can be collected, which makes them susceptible to being destroyed on a whim. This should not happen. All in all, there is a balance to be found between consumer and culture protection on one side, and the right of companies to manage their properties on the other. I do not personally know exactly where the line should be drawn, but I believe it definitely needs to be better regulated than it currently is. Thank you for your time and consideration. With best regards, Alexandre Lazarescu.
